text:
Plant-derived exosome-like nanovesicles (PDENs) have been explored as bioactive molecules for therapeutic applications and drug delivery due to their biological activities and low immunogenicity and stability. However, their application in pharmaceutical field is hampered due to a lack of standard methods for their production, and limited knowledge of their biogenesis and molecular markers. Herein, we for the first time isolated and characterized a new type of PDENs derived from leaves of *Catharanthus roseus* (CLDENs) using an optimized protocol for extraction of apoplastic fluid.

The resulting CLDENs were identified as stable hollow membrane structured vesicles of a diameter of 75 nm and with a pH stability range from 6 to 10 and stability under enzymatic digestion. A detailed lipidomic and differential proteomic analysis led to the identification of ether-phospholipids which constitute more than 36% of the total lipid content and six marker proteins and suggested a biogenetic pathway leading from multivesicular bodies to CLDENs. The functional analysis of CLDENs showed their efficient uptake by immune cells and *in vivo* accumulation in immunological organs. Immunostimulatory properties of CLDENs such as the promotion of M1-type macrophage differentiation and the activation of lymphocytes were demonstrated. Their application to a cyclophosphamide-induced immunosuppressed mouse model resulted in the restoration of depleted white blood cell numbers in the blood and promoted the release of immunosuppressed bone marrow cell cycle arrest. The TNF-α/NF-κB/PU.1 signaling pathway has been identified as the key regulator of the CLDEN-mediated restoration of immune homeostasis and bone marrow function.

To meet the challenge of large scale production we also generated a *C. roseus* cell culture system which resulted in the production of 3 fold more nanovesicles than what could be extracted from a gram of leaf material and nanovesicle composition was unaltered. Therefore CLDENs (ChemotherapY-induced immunoLESIONs-bDening Nanovesicles) present a promising nano-biomaterial that is both stable and biocompatible and which may provide a therapeutic platform to counteract immunosuppression induced by chemotherapy.
